ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Indonesian sambal maut; closest parent 'chili pepper sauce' (FOODON_03301710) used as the nearest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Chilli sauce and similar products) is the closest parent category for sambal maut; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for raw Indonesian chili paste.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to sambal maut or Indonesian chili paste condiment at this specificity level.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard jar serving of Sambal Maut is 220 g; a typical condiment portion at the table is approximately 10–15 g per meal occasion.

nutrient_source.title: Chili sauce, ready-to-serve (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 06164); supplemented with USDA FoodData Central entry for raw bird's eye chili and shallots for raw sambal composition estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA or FAO/INFOODS entry exists for Indonesian sambal maut made from raw chili padi. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for chili sauce (NDB 06164) and raw bird's eye chili pepper (FDC ID 170931), weighted to reflect a predominantly raw chili padi and shallot composition with minimal added salt. Energy and macros are consistent with published FAO/INFOODS SEA data for fresh chili-based condiments. Values represent a best-estimate per 100 g of the finished raw paste.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of chili-based condiments and hot sauces — analogous estimate based on published GI values for fresh chili peppers and chili sauce; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sambal maut. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate based on published GI values for fresh chili peppers (GI ~32, low category) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. Glycemic load per 10 g condiment serving = (32 × 0.78 g carbohydrate per 10 g serving) / 100 ≈ 0.25, rounded to 0.5 for the 15 g typical serving. The low GI is consistent with the high capsaicin, fibre, and water content of raw chili paste and negligible starch.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg/100 g) from added salt in preparation
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit sambal maut to small condiment portions (≤10 g per meal) to keep sodium contribution below 31 mg per serving, and should request reduced-salt preparation where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~32) and low carbohydrate content (~7.8 g/100 g) with high dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sambal maut has a low glycemic index and low glycemic load per condiment serving, making it suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when used in typical condiment quantities; capsaicin may additionally support insulin sensitivity per emerging evidence.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.05 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and high vitamin C content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Sambal maut is very low in saturated fat and cholesterol and provides antioxidant vitamin C; moderate sodium intake from this condiment should be managed within the overall daily sodium budget for individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sambal Maut (literally 'deadly chili paste' in Indonesian/Malay) is an intensely spicy Indonesian chili condiment made primarily from raw bird's eye chili padi, shallots, garlic, salt, and lime juice. The name 'maut' (meaning deadly or lethal) signals extreme heat, and this version is marketed as a Level 6 spice challenge product. The paste is minimally processed — ingredients are blended or hand-pounded raw without cooking — preserving the sharp, fiery, and pungent flavour profile characteristic of fresh chili padi. The texture is coarse to semi-smooth with visible chili seeds and skin fragments. Nutritionally, sambal maut is low in calories (~39 kcal/100 g), very low in fat, and provides modest carbohydrate (~7.8 g/100 g) with dietary fibre (~2.1 g/100 g). It is a notable source of vitamin C (~76 mg/100 g) and contains capsaicin, which has documented anti-inflammatory and metabolic properties. Sodium content (~310 mg/100 g) is moderate due to added salt and warrants attention for hypertensive individuals. The glycemic index is low (~32), making it appropriate for diabetic dietary patterns in condiment-sized portions. Sambal maut is vegan, gluten-free, and halal. It is served as a table condiment alongside Indonesian and Singaporean dishes such as martabak, nasi goreng, and grilled meats. This product is sold in a 220 g jar by Smile Martabak at Funan, Singapore, via field survey.
common_user_queries: Is sambal maut suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry exists for sambal maut in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy chili sauce and raw bird's eye chili entries, adapted to reflect the raw, minimally processed composition of this product. GI is an analogous estimate from published chili pepper GI data. Confidence is medium rather than low because the ingredient composition is well-characterised and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 06164, chili sauce) and raw bird's eye chili pepper (FDC ID 170931), weighted for raw sambal maut composition. No exact approved-source entry exists for this product. 2. Sodium of 310 mg/100 g reflects added salt typical of homemade sambal; actual sodium may vary by recipe. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — raw plant-based paste contains no industrial trans fats; naturally zero by ingredient composition). 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — this is a plant-based condiment with no animal-derived ingredients). 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — raw chili paste contains no vitamin D; confirmed consistent with USDA data for chili-based condiments). 6. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — vegan plant-based product). 7. monounsaturated_fat_g (-1 sentinel not used; value 0.03 estimated from USDA chili pepper lipid profile). 8. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate based on published GI values for fresh chili peppers per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). 9. Serving size of 220 g reflects the full jar as sold; typical condiment portion is 10–15 g. Glycemic load per serving calculated on 15 g typical condiment portion: (32 × 0.078 × 15) / 100 ≈ 0.37, reported as 0.5 (rounded). 10. FoodOn URI FOODON_03301710 and FoodEx2 code A00LC are closest-parent matches; no exact ontology codes exist for sambal maut. 11. Indonesian local name 'Sambal Maut' satisfies the mandatory Indonesian language requirement for Indonesian cuisine per G6.
